content:
Every messy first commit is still sitting there. I’m glad it is.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

GitHub profile page on laptop screen, green contribution graph visible, dark theme, desk setup, soft ambient light, no text overlay, 16:9When I created my GitHub account, I didn’t think about whether to make my repositories public or private. I just made everything public by default, mostly because that’s what the tutorial I was following told me to do. I had no idea that decision would mean every embarrassing, broken, barely-functional piece of code I’d ever write was about to become permanently visible to anyone who looked.

The Decision I Didn’t Really Make
Looking back, “decision” is generous. I didn’t weigh pros and cons. I followed a setup guide that said make your repo public, clicked the button, and moved on with my day. I was focused on getting my first Beecrowd solution pushed up, not on the long-term implications of what would be visible there.
It took a few months before the weight of that decision actually hit me.
I’d push a solution to a problem, feel decent about it, and move on. Then weeks later, I’d go back to check something and look at code I’d written when I genuinely didn’t understand what I was doing. Variable names like x, temp, a1, a2. Logic that worked but made no sense if you tried to read it as a story. Comments that were either nonexistent or embarrassingly basic, the kind you write when you're explaining something to yourself because you don't trust your own understanding yet.
And all of it was just sitting there. Public. Visible to anyone — a recruiter, a more experienced student, a stranger browsing GitHub for inspiration — who happened to click on my profile.

The Specific Discomfort of Being Seen Learning
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Person looking at old code on screen, slightly uncomfortable expression implied through posture, dark room, contemplative mood, no facesThere’s a particular kind of vulnerability in this that’s hard to describe if you haven’t experienced it. It’s not the same as failing a test privately, where only you know the result. It’s not the same as messing something up in a closed environment where the evidence disappears.
This was different. Every step of my learning process — including the steps where I clearly didn’t know what I was doing yet — was sitting in a permanent, dated, timestamped record that anyone could browse through chronologically and watch me figure things out in real time.
I remember feeling genuinely exposed by this for a while. I’d think about deleting some of the earliest repositories. Cleaning up the embarrassing parts. Curating my GitHub the way you’d curate a photo album before showing it to someone — removing the awkward shots, keeping only the flattering ones.
I almost did it more than once. Something kept stopping me, and it took a while to understand what that something actually was.

What Stopped Me From Cleaning It Up
The thing that stopped me, eventually, was a slow realization: the messy early commits weren’t actually evidence that I was bad at this. They were evidence that I’d been doing this for a while, consistently, and had clearly improved.
If I deleted the early stuff, what would be left was a profile that looked like I’d arrived already competent, which wasn’t true, and more importantly, wasn’t useful to anyone, including future me.
A curated, polished-from-day-one profile tells a much less interesting story than a messy, honest one. Anyone can present a handful of clean final projects. Far fewer people can show an actual, visible trajectory — here’s where I started, here’s what I didn’t understand yet, here’s how the code changed as I understood more.
That trajectory is the actual valuable thing. Not any single repository. The shape of the whole timeline.
A polished portfolio shows what you can do today. A messy public history shows how fast you’re capable of changing. The second thing is rarer and more convincing.

What This Actually Looks Like on My Profile Now
Press enter or click to view image in full size

GitHub repository list interface, multiple folders with green commit dots, code timeline visualization, dark theme, no facesIf you look at my GitHub now, you’d see exactly what I’m describing. Early repositories with code that’s functional but unrefined — Beecrowd solutions with minimal structure, simple variable names, the kind of code you write when you’re focused entirely on getting the logic right and haven’t yet developed habits around readability or style.
Then, moving forward chronologically, you’d see the same kind of problems being solved with more structure. Better naming. Comments that explain reasoning rather than just narrating what a line does. A growing awareness that code isn’t just for the computer to execute — it’s for a human, possibly a future version of myself, to read and understand later.
None of this was deliberate documentation. I never sat down and thought, “I should structure my commits to show growth.” It happened naturally, as a side effect of actually getting better and just continuing to push code publicly the whole time.
That’s the part that makes it feel authentic rather than performed. Nobody can fake a multi-month trajectory of gradual, consistent improvement. You either have one, or you don’t.

Why This Matters More Than I Initially Realized
I used to think the value of a public GitHub was mainly about having projects to show recruiters — a portfolio, essentially, in the same category as a resume or a LinkedIn profile.
I’ve come to think the value is actually different and bigger than that.
It proves consistency in a way that’s hard to fake.
Anyone can claim they’ve been learning to code for six months. A GitHub history with commits spread across that entire period, showing actual activity rather than a sudden burst right before a job application, is much harder evidence to dismiss.
It shows how you think, not just what you produce.
Reading someone’s commit history — the false starts, the refactors, the moments where you can see them realize a better approach mid-project — tells a more complete story than a single finished snapshot. It shows process, not just output.
It removes the pressure to be perfect before sharing.
Once you accept that your public profile will show imperfect work, you stop hesitating before pushing something just because it isn’t polished yet. This alone made me ship things faster and more often, because I’d already let go of the fear of being seen mid-progress.
It builds an honest relationship with your own growth.
Going back and looking at code you wrote six months ago and being able to clearly identify what you’d do differently now is one of the most concrete ways to actually feel your own progress. A curated, deleted-and-replaced history doesn’t give you that same evidence.

The Recruiter Angle, Specifically
I’ve heard enough from people further along in their careers to know this matters specifically for job searching, even though that wasn’t my original motivation for keeping things public.
A recruiter or hiring manager looking at a junior candidate’s GitHub isn’t expecting to see senior-level code. They’re expecting to see exactly what a learning developer’s code looks like — and what they’re actually evaluating is whether there’s a visible trajectory of improvement, whether the person ships consistently, and whether the commit messages and structure suggest someone who’s developing good habits over time.
A profile with three polished, possibly recently cleaned-up projects and nothing else tells a thin story. A profile with a long, visible, slightly messy history that clearly shows someone getting better month over month tells a much more convincing one — especially for a first or second year student who isn’t expected to already be excellent.
Consistency is the actual signal recruiters are looking for in a junior candidate’s GitHub. Perfection isn’t, because nobody expects it yet.

What I’d Tell Someone Deciding Whether to Go Public
If you’re a student right now deciding whether to make your repositories public or keep them private until they’re “ready,” here’s what I’d say based on going through this myself.
There is no point where your early work will feel ready. The standard you’re holding yourself to keeps moving as you improve, which means if you wait for your code to feel good enough to show, you’ll wait indefinitely.
The discomfort of having imperfect work visible fades faster than you expect, and it fades specifically because you keep producing more work that makes the earlier stuff look like a natural starting point rather than a permanent embarrassment.
Make it public from the start. Not because the code will look good — it won’t, not at first — but because the visible trajectory from not-good to better is the actual asset you’re building, and it only exists if the not-good part is still there to compare against.

Where I Am Now
My GitHub still has every awkward early commit sitting exactly where I left it. I haven’t deleted anything. I occasionally still cringe slightly looking back at some of it.
But I’ve stopped seeing that cringe as a problem to fix and started seeing it as proof that the version of me writing this article is meaningfully different from the version of me who pushed that first messy solution. That difference is the whole point.
If you’re earlier in this process than I am and your code right now feels too embarrassing to share publicly, I’d gently push back on that instinct. The embarrassment is temporary. The visible record of growth, once it exists, is permanent and genuinely valuable — to recruiters, sure, but honestly, mostly to you.
Don’t wait until your code looks good to make it public. Make it public so you can watch it become good, in front of anyone who’s paying attention.

Saharia Hassan Safin — CSE student at Daffodil International University, currently building in public and learning cybersecurity along the way. Find the messy, honest history on GitHub or connect on LinkedIn.
